schools.summary: The writer gathering up his argument: 'now of the things which we have spoken this is the sum (kephalaion — the chief point, the crowning summary)' — the central point of all said about the priesthood now stated; 'we have (now possess) such an high priest' — believers actually having a high priest of the surpassing dignity just described (the Melchizedek priest, ch. 7); 'who is set (sat down, enthroned) on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty (God) in the heavens' — unlike the earthly priests who stood to minister, our high priest seated, enthroned at God's right hand, his finished work and royal session (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: our high priest does not stand to minister, as the earthly priests, but is seated enthroned at God's right hand in heaven — a token that his sacrifice is finished and his royal dignity supreme, far above the Levitical priesthood.

themes: of the things which we have spoken this is the sum: We have such an high priest
who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens
the sum of the argument: an enthroned high priest in heaven
seated at God's right hand

schools.summary: Christ's sphere of service: 'a minister (leitourgos — one who performs sacred service) of the sanctuary (ta hagia — the holy place), and of the true (alethinos — real, genuine, as opposed to the typical) tabernacle' — Christ ministering not in the earthly tent but in the true, heavenly sanctuary (heaven itself, or the heavenly sanctuary, of which the earthly was only a copy, the reality not the shadow); 'which the Lord pitched (set up, erected), and not man' — unlike the Mosaic tabernacle pitched by human hands, the heavenly sanctuary God's own making, its divine origin marking its superiority (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: A minister of the sanctuary, and of the true tabernacle
which the Lord pitched, and not man
Christ a minister of the true heavenly tabernacle
the real sanctuary, God's own making

schools.summary: The principle: 'for every high priest is ordained (appointed, constituted) to offer gifts and sacrifices' — the essential office of a high priest being to offer, the very purpose of his appointment (cf. 5:1); 'wherefore it is of necessity (necessary) that this man (Christ) have somewhat also to offer' — since every high priest must offer, Christ too, being a high priest, must have an offering, unable to be a priest without a sacrifice; Christ's 'somewhat to offer' being, as the epistle shows, himself, his own blood (9:12, 14), unlike the earthly priests who offered animals (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices
it is of necessity that this man have somewhat also to offer
Christ must have somewhat to offer
his offering being himself

schools.summary: The reasoning: 'for if he were on earth, he should not be a priest (he would not even be a priest at all)' — Christ's priesthood not of the earthly, Levitical kind, for on earth he would not qualify, being of Judah not Levi (7:14); 'seeing that there are (already) priests that offer gifts according to the law' — the earthly priestly office fully occupied by the Levitical priests appointed by the law, with no room or need for another earthly priest; Christ's priesthood belonging not to earth but to heaven, ministering in the true tabernacle above (v.2), of a different and higher order (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if he were on earth, he should not be a priest
seeing that there are priests that offer gifts according to the law
Christ's priesthood heavenly, not earthly
of a higher order than the Levitical

schools.summary: The nature of the Levitical service: 'who serve unto the example (hypodeigma — a copy, sketch, representation) and shadow (skia — a faint outline) of heavenly things' — the earthly priests ministering at a copy and shadow of the true, heavenly realities, their service figurative and pointing beyond itself; 'as Moses was admonished (warned, divinely instructed) of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern (typos — model, archetype) shewed to thee in the mount' (Exodus 25:40) — God giving Moses a pattern, the earthly tabernacle made after a heavenly model, the very command proving the earthly a copy of a heavenly original (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things
make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount
the earthly priests serving a shadow of heavenly things
the earthly a copy of the heavenly

schools.summary: The contrast: 'but now hath he obtained (attained, received) a more excellent (superior, surpassing) ministry' — Christ's priestly ministry far superior to the Levitical, as the heavenly reality surpasses the earthly shadow; 'by how much (in proportion as) also he is the mediator (mesites — the go-between, one who acts between two parties to reconcile) of a better covenant (diatheke), which was established (enacted, legally founded) upon better promises' — Christ's ministry superior because the covenant he mediates is superior, the new covenant resting on richer promises (forgiveness, the law written on the heart, the knowledge of God, vv.10-12) than the old (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: now hath he obtained a more excellent ministry
he is the mediator of a better covenant, which was established upon better promises
Christ's more excellent ministry as mediator of a better covenant
the new covenant on better promises

schools.summary: The reasoning: 'for if that first covenant had been faultless (amemptos — without defect, perfect), then should no place have been sought for the second' — had the old covenant been perfect, accomplishing all that was needed, there would have been no occasion for a new one, the very promise of a second proving the first deficient; the first covenant not 'faultless' (not in itself sinful, but defective, weak, unable to perfect, cf. 7:18-19), its fault lying in its inability to take away sin and bring men fully to God; God's seeking place for a second (the new covenant of Jeremiah 31, v.8) itself the verdict on the old (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: if that first covenant had been faultless, then should no place have been sought for the second
the first covenant's fault required a second
the old covenant defective and weak
the promise of a new covenant the verdict on the old

schools.summary: God himself found fault: 'for finding fault with them (the people, or the covenant)' — interpreters differing whether the fault is found with the people (who broke the covenant) or with the covenant itself, the Greek allowing 'with them,' pointing to the people's failure under the old covenant which revealed its weakness; the great prophecy quoted (Jeremiah 31:31-34, the longest OT quotation in the NT): 'Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make (conclude, ratify) a new covenant (kaine diatheke — a new arrangement, fresh in kind) with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah' — God promising a new covenant to replace the old, with his people (the divided nation, fulfilled in the spiritual Israel, the church) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: finding fault with them, he saith, Behold, the days come
I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah
God's promise of a new covenant
finding fault with the old

schools.summary: The contrast: 'not according to (not like) the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt' — the new covenant not a renewal of the Sinai covenant made at the Exodus but different in kind; 'I took them by the hand' a figure of tender care, like a father leading a child, in the deliverance from Egypt; 'because they continued not (did not abide) in my covenant, and I regarded them not (disregarded them, turned away)' — the people breaking the covenant by disobedience, and God in consequence withdrawing his favour, the old covenant failing through human unfaithfulness and so showing the need of a better (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the old covenant was broken by the people's disobedience, and so could not save; the new covenant, written inwardly on the heart, supplies what the old, dependent on human keeping, could not — God's own work establishing what man's failure had undone.

themes: not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers... to lead them out of the land of Egypt
because they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not
the new covenant unlike the broken Sinai covenant
the old covenant failing through human unfaithfulness

schools.summary: The first great promise of the new covenant: 'I will put my laws into their mind (their understanding), and write them in their hearts (their inmost being)' — unlike the old covenant, written on tables of stone and external, the new covenant writing the law within, on the mind and heart, an inward, transforming law; 'and I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people' — the heart of the covenant relationship, the mutual belonging of God and his people, God owning them and they being his (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Cyril): the law written on the heart is the work of the Spirit in regeneration — God inscribes his will inwardly, so that the believer obeys not by external compulsion but from a renewed heart; this inward principle of obedience is the great superiority of the new covenant over the old, which commanded but could not enable.

themes: I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts
I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people
the new covenant: the law written on the heart
the inward, regenerating covenant

schools.summary: The second great promise: 'and they shall not teach every man his neighbour, and every man his brother, saying, Know the Lord: for all shall know me, from the least to the greatest' — under the new covenant, the knowledge of God not confined to a few teachers but shared by all God's people, every member (high and low, least and greatest, without exception) knowing the Lord with a real, inward, saving knowledge (not merely external instruction but a personal acquaintance with God), the new covenant bringing what the old, with its external teaching, did not universally impart (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: they shall not teach every man his neighbour... Know the Lord: for all shall know me
from the least to the greatest
the new covenant promise that all shall know the Lord
universal, inward knowledge of God

schools.summary: The crowning promise, the basis of all the rest: 'for I will be merciful to (gracious toward) their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more (will I no longer hold against them)' — God's mercy toward the sins of his people the foundation of the new covenant, his forgiveness so complete that he treats their sins as if forgotten, never bringing them up again (a full and final pardon, unlike the old covenant's yearly remembrance of sins, 10:3); this forgiveness ('for') the ground of the whole new covenant, from which the inward law and the knowledge of God spring (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more
the new covenant promise of mercy and forgotten sins
full and final forgiveness
pardon the foundation of the covenant's blessings

schools.summary: The writer's inference from the word 'new': 'in that he saith, A new (kaine) covenant, he hath made (declared, treated as) the first old (palaios — antiquated, obsolete)' — the very calling of one covenant 'new' implying the other is old, superseded, God's word in Jeremiah pronouncing the old covenant obsolete; 'now that which decayeth (is becoming obsolete) and waxeth old (grows aged, like a worn garment) is ready (near) to vanish away (disappear, be abolished)' — the old covenant, declared old, in process of passing away and on the verge of disappearing (written before AD 70, perhaps hinting at the coming destruction of the temple and the final passing of the old order) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old
that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away
the old covenant made obsolete and ready to vanish
superseded by the new
